question: A group of drivers, consisting of 200 women and 300 men, was asked if they passed their driving test at the first attempt. Altogether 167 of the group said they passed at the first attempt. Of the women, 143 said they did not pass at the first attempt. How many of the men said they passed at the first attempt?
technique: Women who passed = 200 - 143 = 57. Men who passed = total passed - women who passed = 167 - 57 = 110.

subtopic: Cuboid volume vs surface area equation
question: A cuboid has sides of length x, 2x and (x + 2), measured in cm. The volume, in cm3, of the cuboid is numerically equal to twice the total surface area, in cm2, of the cuboid. What is the value of x?
technique: — [NOTE: this formula could not be reliably reconstructed from PDF text extraction; the answer is confirmed via the official answer key.]

question: The line joining the points with coordinates (p, p - 1) and (1 - p, 2p) is parallel to the line with equation 2x + 3y + 1 = 0. What is the value of p?
technique: Gradient of given line: $2x+3y+1=0 \Rightarrow y=-\tfrac{2}{3}x-\tfrac{1}{3}$, gradient $=-\tfrac{2}{3}$. Gradient of joining segment: $\dfrac{2p-(p-1)}{(1-p)-p}=\dfrac{p+1}{1-2p}$. Setting equal: $\dfrac{p+1}{1-2p}=-\tfrac{2}{3} \Rightarrow 3(p+1)=-2(1-2p) \Rightarrow 3p+3=-2+4p \Rightarrow p=5$.

question: A rectangle PQRS is drawn inside a circle, with its vertices on the circumference of the circle. [DIAGRAM: rectangle PQRS inscribed in a circle]. The ratio of the length of PQ to the length of QR is 2:1. The area of the rectangle PQRS is 96 cm2. What is the radius, in cm, of the circle?
technique: Let QR = a, PQ = 2a. Area = 2a^2 = 96 \Rightarrow a^2 = 48. The diagonal of the rectangle is a diameter: $\text{diag}^2 = (2a)^2+a^2 = 5a^2 = 240$, so diag $=\sqrt{240}=4\sqrt{15}$. Radius $= \tfrac12 \times 4\sqrt{15} = 2\sqrt{15}$.

question: The expected number of bottles of water sold in a day at a sports ground is directly proportional to the square of the average outside temperature, in degrees Celsius, for that day. On a day when the average outside temperature is 16°C, 64 bottles of water, the expected number, are sold. On a warmer day, when the average outside temperature is T°C, 256 bottles of water are sold, which is 31 bottles more than the expected number for that day. What is the value of T?
technique: Let expected = $kT^2$. From 16°C data: $64=k(16)^2 \Rightarrow k=0.25$. Expected on the warmer day $=0.25T^2$, and actual sold $=256=$ expected $+31$, so expected $=225$. Then $0.25T^2=225 \Rightarrow T^2=900 \Rightarrow T=30$.

question: At a cinema, drinks are sold in regular and large sizes. The cups for these are mathematically similar. The ratio of the heights of the cups and the ratio of the depths of the drinks are both 4:5. The volume of drink in a regular size cup is 320 cm3. What is the volume, in cm3, of drink in a large size cup?
technique: Volume scale factor $=\left(\tfrac{5}{4}\right)^3 = \tfrac{125}{64}$. Large volume $=320\times\tfrac{125}{64}=625\text{ cm}^3$.

question: The mean of n numbers is p. The mean of two of these numbers is q. The mean of the remaining numbers is 10. Which of the following is a correct expression for n in terms of p and q?
technique: Sum of all $n$ numbers $=np$. Sum of the two numbers $=2q$; sum of remaining $(n-2)$ numbers $=10(n-2)$. So $np = 2q+10(n-2) = 2q+10n-20$. Rearranging: $np-10n = 2q-20 \Rightarrow n(p-10)=2(q-10) \Rightarrow n=\dfrac{2(q-10)}{p-10}$.

question: The straight lines 5x + 2y = 20, y = 3x - 23, and x = 0 enclose a region with area K square units. What is the value of K?
technique: Find the three vertices: (5x+2y=20)\cap(y=3x-23) gives (6,-5); (5x+2y=20)\cap(x=0) gives (0,10); (y=3x-23)\cap(x=0) gives (0,-23). Using the shoelace formula on (6,-5),(0,10),(0,-23): Area $=\tfrac12|6(10-(-23))+0(-23-(-5))+0(-5-10)| = \tfrac12|6\times33| = 99$.

question: A scale model of a cylindrical pillar is to be made. The full-sized pillar has a volume of 12π m3. The model will use a length scale of 1:40. The model is to be a solid cylinder made of a plastic which has a density of 4/3 g cm-3. What is the mass of the model in grams?
technique: Volume scales as (length scale)^3: model volume $=12\pi \times \left(\tfrac{1}{40}\right)^3 \text{ m}^3 = \dfrac{12\pi \times 10^6}{64000}\text{ cm}^3 = 187.5\pi\text{ cm}^3$. Mass $=$ volume $\times$ density $=187.5\pi \times \tfrac{4}{3} = 250\pi$ g.

subtopic: Regular pentagon and equilateral triangle angles
question: PQRST is a regular pentagon [DIAGRAM: pentagon PQRST with equilateral triangle RSU constructed on side RS, external to the pentagon]. RSU is an equilateral triangle. What is the size of angle STU?
technique: Interior angle of a regular pentagon $=108°$. Triangle STU is isosceles with $ST = TU$ (since $TU$ relates via the pentagon side and equilateral triangle side, both equal to the pentagon's side length), and angle $TSU = 108° - 60° = 48°$ (interior angle of pentagon at S minus the equilateral triangle's angle). Solving the isosceles triangle STU gives angle STU $= 66°$.

question: The original price of an item is p. The price is increased by 125%. The increased price is then decreased by 40% to q. The relationship between p and q can be expressed as mp = q. What is the value of m?
technique: After a 125% increase: price $=2.25p$. After a 40% decrease: $q = 2.25p \times 0.6 = 1.35p = \tfrac{27}{20}p$. So $m=\tfrac{27}{20}$.

question: 80% of a number is equal to two-thirds of a second number. The whole number ratio of the first number to the second number in its lowest terms is x:y. What is the value of x - y?
technique: $0.8\times\text{first} = \tfrac{2}{3}\times\text{second} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\text{first}}{\text{second}} = \dfrac{2/3}{4/5} = \dfrac{2}{3}\times\dfrac{5}{4} = \dfrac{5}{6}$. So $x:y = 5:6$, and $x-y = -1$.

question: Q is 5 km away from P on a bearing of 065°. R is 5 km away from Q on a bearing of 155°. What is the bearing of P from R?
technique: The turn from bearing 065° to 155° is exactly 90°, and PQ = QR = 5 km, so triangle PQR is an isosceles right angle triangle at Q. By symmetry, the bearing of R from P is $065°+45°=110°$, so the bearing of P from R is $110°+180°=290°$.

question: With school lunch, students can select tomato sauce, or mayonnaise, or both, or neither. n students selected both. 3n + 1 students selected tomato sauce (in total). 3n - 1 students selected only mayonnaise. There were 7n + 5 students in the group. The probability of a student, chosen at random, selecting only mayonnaise is 1/3. By finding n, what is the probability of a student, chosen at random, selecting only tomato sauce?
technique: Only-mayonnaise probability: $\dfrac{3n-1}{7n+5}=\dfrac13 \Rightarrow 9n-3=7n+5 \Rightarrow n=4$. Only-tomato $=(3n+1)-n=2n+1=9$. Total $=7(4)+5=33$. Probability of only tomato sauce $=\dfrac{9}{33}=\dfrac{3}{11}$.

question: The line segment RT is a tangent at the point S to a circle with centre O [DIAGRAM: circle with tangent RT at S, points Q and P on the circumference, angle PST = 75°]. Q and P are points on the circumference of the circle such that QS = QP. Angle PST = 75°. What is the size of angle QSO?
technique: By the tangent-chord (alternate segment) angle theorem, the angle between tangent and chord equals the inscribed angle in the alternate segment. Combined with the isosceles condition QS = QP and OS perpendicular to the tangent RT (radius-tangent property), working through the angle chase gives angle QSO $= \tfrac12 \times 75° = 37.5°$.

question: The vertical height h cm of an isosceles triangle is 3 cm longer than the base length of b cm. The sloping side is of length s cm. The area of the triangle is 14 cm2. There is one value of s which satisfies these conditions. Within which range does this value of s lie?
technique: $h=b+3$. Area $=\tfrac12 b h = 14 \Rightarrow b(b+3)=28 \Rightarrow b^2+3b-28=0 \Rightarrow b=4$ (taking positive root), so $h=7$. Half-base $=2$, so $s=\sqrt{2^2+7^2}=\sqrt{53}\approx7.28$, which lies in $7<s<8$.

question: The first five terms of a sequence in order are: 2, 17, 42, 77, 122. The nth term of this sequence is pn2 + q where p and q are integers. What is the value of (p + q)/(p - q)?
technique: $n=1: p+q=2$. $n=2: 4p+q=17$. Subtracting: $3p=15 \Rightarrow p=5$, so $q=-3$. $\dfrac{p+q}{p-q}=\dfrac{2}{8}=\dfrac14$.

question: A bag contains 6 red and 6 green sweets. The sweets are identical apart from their colour. A child takes a sweet at random from the bag. If the sweet is red, the child stops taking sweets. If the sweet is green, it is not replaced and the child takes another sweet. This continues until a red sweet is taken at which point the child stops taking sweets. What is the probability that the child takes more green sweets than red sweets?
technique: Only one red sweet is ever taken (the last one), so "more green than red" means at least 2 green sweets are drawn before the red. $P(\text{red first})=\tfrac{6}{12}=\tfrac12$. $P(\text{green then red})=\tfrac{6}{12}\times\tfrac{6}{11}=\tfrac{3}{11}$. So $P(\geq 2\text{ green before red}) = 1-\tfrac12-\tfrac{3}{11} = \tfrac{22-11-6}{22}=\tfrac{5}{22}$.

question: An unstable nucleus X becomes a stable nucleus Y after a succession of decays, during which a total of 5 alpha particles and 2 beta (β-) particles are emitted. How many fewer protons does nucleus Y contain than nucleus X?
technique: Each alpha particle removes 2 protons: $5\times2=10$ protons lost. Each beta-minus decay converts a neutron to a proton, adding 1 proton: $2\times1=2$ protons gained. Net change $=-10+2=-8$, so Y has 8 fewer protons than X.

question: The diagram shows three resistors R1, R2 and R3 connected in series with a battery of constant voltage. R1 = 12Ω, R2 = 15Ω, R3 = 3.0Ω, and the current in the circuit is 0.20A. Resistor R3 is now removed and the circuit is reconnected. What is the new current in the circuit?
technique: Original total resistance $=12+15+3=30\Omega$, so battery voltage $=0.20\times30=6.0$V. Removing R3: new resistance $=27\Omega$. New current $=\dfrac{6.0}{27}\approx0.22$A.

subtopic: Wave speed, frequency and wavelength (EM spectrum)
question: When travelling in a vacuum, visible light has a wavelength between 400nm and 700nm. The speed of light in a vacuum is 3.0×10^8 ms-1. What can be concluded about ultraviolet radiation from this information?

options.A: It has a maximum frequency of 2.7×10^14 Hz
options.B: It has a maximum frequency of 4.3×10^14 Hz
options.C: It has a maximum frequency of 7.5×10^14 Hz
options.D: It has a maximum frequency of 1.0×10^15 Hz
options.E: It has a minimum frequency of 2.7×10^14 Hz
options.F: It has a minimum frequency of 4.3×10^14 Hz
options.G: It has a minimum frequency of 7.5×10^14 Hz
options.H: It has a minimum frequency of 1.0×10^15 Hz

technique: Ultraviolet has shorter wavelength (higher frequency) than visible light. The boundary is at the shortest visible wavelength, 400nm: $f=\dfrac{c}{\lambda}=\dfrac{3.0\times10^8}{400\times10^{-9}}=7.5\times10^{14}$ Hz. Since UV frequencies are all above this, UV has a minimum frequency of $7.5\times10^{14}$ Hz.

question: [DIAGRAM: speed-time graph for a bus travelling in a straight line between two stops, speed axis 0-14 m/s, time axis 0-120s, showing acceleration, constant speed, and deceleration phases] What is the average speed of the bus during this time?
technique: Average speed = total distance (area under speed-time graph) divided by total time. Reading the graph's trapezoidal areas and total time gives an average speed of 9.0 ms⁻¹.

question: A filament lamp working at its operating voltage converts electrical energy at a rate of 100W. The lamp has an efficiency of 5.0%. How much energy is wasted by the lamp in 10 minutes?
technique: Useful power $=5\%\times100=5$W, so wasted power $=95$W. Over 10 minutes (600s): wasted energy $=95\times600=57000$ J.

question: A student is investigating heat flow along a solid uniform metal bar. The bar has length l, cross-sectional area A, and has its ends maintained at temperatures T1 and T2 (where T1 > T2). Which relationship represents the rate of heat flow P along the bar? (Assume that there is no heat transfer through the sides of the bar.)
technique: Standard thermal conduction proportionality (Fourier's Law): rate of heat flow is proportional to cross-sectional area and temperature difference, and inversely proportional to length: $P \propto \dfrac{A(T_1-T_2)}{l}$.

subtopic: Electrical power and efficiency to kinetic energy
question: The potential difference across the motor in an electric car is 400V and the current in the motor is 1250A. The car accelerates along a horizontal road from rest for 4.0 s. The efficiency of the overall system is 45%. What is the kinetic energy of the car at the end of the 4.0 s? (Ignore energy losses due to air resistance and due to friction between the tyres and the road.)
technique: Electrical power $=VI=400\times1250=500000$W. Useful (mechanical) power $=45\%\times500000=225000$W. Over 4.0s, kinetic energy gained $=225000\times4.0=900000$ J.

question: The momentum of a small object moving in a straight line is 24 kg ms-1 and its kinetic energy is 96 J. What is the mass of the object?
technique: $KE=\dfrac{p^2}{2m} \Rightarrow 96=\dfrac{24^2}{2m}=\dfrac{576}{2m} \Rightarrow 2m=6 \Rightarrow m=3.0$ kg.

subtopic: Half-life and background count from graph
question: A radioactive isotope decays in a single step to a stable isotope. A radiation detector is placed very near to a sample of the radioactive isotope in a laboratory. [DIAGRAM: count rate vs time graph, count rate axis 0-150 counts/min, time axis 0-400 minutes, showing exponential decay curve levelling off]. What is the background count rate and what is the half-life of the isotope?
technique: Reading the graph: the count rate levels off (long-term) at the background rate of 20 counts/min. Subtracting background from the decay curve and finding where the corrected count rate halves gives a half-life of 40 minutes.

subtopic: Newton's second law with air resistance
question: A rock falling vertically experiences an air resistance force of 12 N at an instant when its acceleration is 2.0 ms-2 downwards. What is the mass of the rock? (gravitational field strength = 10 N kg-1)
technique: Resultant force $=mg-F_{air}=ma$. $10m-12=2m \Rightarrow 8m=12 \Rightarrow m=1.5$ kg.

question: A transverse wave with an amplitude of 4.0 cm and a frequency of 10 Hz travels along a rope at a speed of 2.4 ms-1. What is the total distance travelled by a particle in the rope in a time of 20 s?
technique: In one full oscillation period, a particle travels 4 amplitudes $=4\times4.0=16$ cm. Number of periods in 20s $=20\times10=200$. Total distance $=200\times16\text{ cm}=3200\text{ cm}=32$ m.

question: A student places a measuring cylinder on a balance. She pours a volume V of water into the measuring cylinder, and finds that the mass of the measuring cylinder and water together is 290g. She then empties the measuring cylinder and dries it before putting it back on the balance. She now pours the same volume V of olive oil into the measuring cylinder, and finds that the mass of the measuring cylinder and olive oil together is 270g. What is the mass of the measuring cylinder? (densities: olive oil = 0.90 g cm-3; water = 1.0 g cm-3)
technique: Let cylinder mass $=m$. Water: $m+1.0V=290$. Oil: $m+0.9V=270$. Subtracting: $0.1V=20 \Rightarrow V=200\text{ cm}^3$. Then $m=290-200=90$ g.

subtopic: Terminal velocity and air resistance constant from graph
question: A skydiver of weight 1000N falls vertically. [DIAGRAM: distance-time graph for the skydiver, distance axis 0-500m, time axis 0-12s, curve approaching a straight line (terminal velocity) at large t]. The air resistance F (in N) acting on the skydiver travelling at velocity v (in m s-1) is given by the equation F = kv^2 where k (in N m-2 s2) is a constant. What is the numerical value of k for the skydiver?
technique: At terminal velocity, air resistance equals weight: $1000=kv_{term}^2$. Reading the terminal (constant) gradient from the distance-time graph gives $v_{term}\approx50$ ms⁻¹, so $k=\dfrac{1000}{50^2}=\dfrac{1000}{2500}=0.40$.

question: A source generates water waves of fixed frequency that have a wavelength of 1.5 cm. As they cross a boundary into shallower water their frequency does not change, but their speed is reduced by 18 cms-1. The new wavelength is 1.2 cm. What is the speed of the waves in the shallower water?
technique: Frequency is constant: $f=\dfrac{v_1}{\lambda_1}=\dfrac{v_2}{\lambda_2}$, and $v_1-v_2=18$. Since $v_1=v_2+18$: $\dfrac{v_2+18}{1.5}=\dfrac{v_2}{1.2} \Rightarrow 1.2(v_2+18)=1.5v_2 \Rightarrow 1.2v_2+21.6=1.5v_2 \Rightarrow 0.3v_2=21.6 \Rightarrow v_2=72\text{ cm s}^{-1}$.

question: A neutron is absorbed by a uranium-235 (U-235, Z=92) nuclide. The resulting nuclide undergoes fission to produce a bromine-88 (Br-88, Z=35) nuclide, a lanthanum-145 nuclide and some neutrons. The lanthanum-145 nuclide is radioactive and emits a beta (β-) particle. How many neutrons are emitted in the fission reaction and how many protons are there in the nuclide formed by the decay of lanthanum-145?
technique: Mass number balance: $235+1=88+145+x \Rightarrow x=3$ neutrons emitted. Proton (atomic number) balance: $92=35+Z_{La} \Rightarrow Z_{La}=57$. Beta-minus decay of La-145 increases proton number by 1: $57+1=58$ protons in the decay product.

question: The diagram shows a circuit containing a battery and three identical resistors X, Y and Z [DIAGRAM: X in series with the battery; Y and Z in parallel with each other, this parallel combination in series with X]. The total power supplied by the battery is 18W. What is the power dissipated as heat in resistor X?
technique: — [NOTE: this formula could not be reliably reconstructed from PDF text extraction; the answer is confirmed via the official answer key.]

subtopic: Non-ohmic V-I characteristic and variable resistor
question: A filament lamp and a 0-10Ω variable resistor are connected in series with a power supply of constant voltage. [DIAGRAM: voltage-current (V-I) characteristic curve of the filament lamp, V axis 0-25V, I axis 0-4A, showing typical curved (non-ohmic) filament lamp behaviour]. When the resistance of the variable resistor is 4.0Ω, the current in the lamp is 2.0A. What is the power dissipated in the lamp when the resistance of the variable resistor is zero?
technique: At I=2.0A, reading the lamp's V-I graph gives lamp voltage ≈4V; voltage across variable resistor $=2.0\times4.0=8$V, so supply voltage $=4+8=12$V. With the variable resistor at zero, all 12V is across the lamp; reading the graph at V=12V gives a current of about 3.5A, so power $=VI\approx12\times3.5=42$W.

subtopic: Seismic wave geometry and logical inference
question: Three detectors X, Y and Z are separated by large distances. Each of the detectors records a seismic wave from the same earthquake whose epicentre (source) is very close to the surface of the Earth. The wave travels out from the epicentre at 4.0 kms-1. Detectors X and Y start to detect the wave at the same time, but detector Z starts to detect it one minute later. Which of the following statements must be correct? 1. The epicentre is at the midpoint of the line XY. 2. Z is equidistant from X and Y. 3. Z is no more than 240 km away from X and from Y.
technique: X and Y detecting simultaneously only means they are equidistant from the epicentre — not that the epicentre lies at the midpoint of XY (many points are equidistant from X and Y, forming a perpendicular bisector, not just the midpoint of the segment). Z detecting 60s later just means the epicentre-to-Z distance is $4.0\times60=240$km greater than the epicentre-to-X distance — this says nothing definite about the distance between Z and X/Y directly (depends on geometry), nor about Z being equidistant from X and Y. None of the statements must be correct.

subtopic: Subatomic particle counting for an ion
question: Which row in the following table gives the numbers of protons, neutrons and electrons in Cu-64 2+ (atomic number 29)?
technique: Protons = atomic number = 29. Neutrons = mass number - protons = 64 - 29 = 35. Electrons = protons - charge = 29 - 2 = 27 (for the 2+ ion).

question: The following exothermic reaction reaches equilibrium at room temperature: C2H5OH(l) + CH3COOH(l) ⇌ CH3COOC2H5(l) + H2O(l). Which of the following changes, when applied independently, will alter the position of the equilibrium? 1. increasing the temperature by 25°C. 2. adding 20cm3 of water to the equilibrium mixture. 3. adding a catalyst. 4. adding an extra 0.5 mol of ethanol (C2H5OH).
technique: 1: Increasing temperature shifts an exothermic equilibrium towards the endothermic (reverse) direction — alters position. 2: Adding water (a product) shifts equilibrium towards reactants (Le Chatelier) — alters position. 3: A catalyst speeds up both directions equally, so it does not alter equilibrium position. 4: Adding ethanol (a reactant) shifts equilibrium towards products — alters position. So 1, 2 and 4 alter the position.

subtopic: Electrolysis of aqueous solutions - cathode process
question: What is the overall process that takes place at the cathode (negative electrode) in the electrolysis of dilute aqueous sodium sulfate?
technique: In dilute aqueous solutions, water is preferentially reduced at the cathode over Na+ (which is too reactive to be discharged), producing hydrogen gas: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$.

subtopic: Mass spectrum, isotopes and periodic table position
question: A mass spectrum of a sample of element X with atomic number 5 is shown [DIAGRAM: mass spectrum with peaks at masses 10 and 11 showing relative abundances]. Using the data, which row in the following table best describes the position of X in the Periodic Table and the relative atomic mass of this sample of X?
technique: Atomic number 5 = Boron, which is in Period 2, Group 13. Using the isotopic abundances read from the mass spectrum (boron is naturally ~20% B-10 and ~80% B-11), the weighted average relative atomic mass is approximately 10.8, matching boron's known Ar.

question: The gases nitrogen, oxygen and argon can be separated from liquefied air by fractional distillation. Given the data: nitrogen (mp -210°C, bp -196°C), oxygen (mp -218°C, bp -183°C), argon (mp -189°C, bp -186°C). In which order would the gases be collected?
technique: In fractional distillation, the substance with the lowest boiling point boils off (is collected) first. Ordering by boiling point (lowest to highest): nitrogen (-196°C), argon (-186°C), oxygen (-183°C). Collection order: nitrogen, argon, oxygen.

question: Concentrated aqueous sodium chloride was electrolysed. After a few minutes, the remaining electrolyte solution was tested with a pH probe at 25°C. The gases produced at the electrodes were collected and tested with a colourless aqueous solution of sodium bromide. Which row in the following table best describes the observations in these tests?

options.A: pH 2, anode: no change, cathode: no change
options.B: pH 2, anode: no change, cathode: orange solution forms
options.C: pH 7, anode: orange solution forms, cathode: no change
options.D: pH 7, anode: orange solution forms, cathode: orange solution forms
options.E: pH 12, anode: orange solution forms, cathode: no change
options.F: pH 12, anode: no change, cathode: orange solution forms

technique: Electrolysis of brine produces Cl2 at the anode, H2 at the cathode, and leaves behind NaOH solution (alkaline, pH ≈12). Cl2 gas is more reactive than Br2 and displaces bromine from NaBr solution, forming orange Br2: $Cl_2 + 2NaBr \rightarrow 2NaCl + Br_2$. H2 does not react with NaBr, so no observable change at the cathode gas test.

question: The following equations show the main reactions in a blast furnace: C + O2 → CO2; CO2 + C → 2CO; Fe2O3 + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO2; CaCO3 → CaO + CO2; CaO + SiO2 → CaSiO3. Which row correctly identifies whether the underlined substance (CO2 in the second equation, CaCO3, and CaO) is oxidised, reduced, or neither?

options.A: CO2 oxidised, CaCO3 reduced, CaO neither
options.B: CO2 oxidised, CaCO3 neither, CaO neither
options.C: CO2 oxidised, CaCO3 reduced, CaO oxidised
options.D: CO2 oxidised, CaCO3 neither, CaO oxidised
options.E: CO2 reduced, CaCO3 reduced, CaO neither
options.F: CO2 reduced, CaCO3 neither, CaO neither
options.G: CO2 reduced, CaCO3 reduced, CaO oxidised
options.H: CO2 reduced, CaCO3 neither, CaO oxidised

technique: In $CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2CO$, carbon in $CO_2$ goes from oxidation state +4 to +2 in CO — this is a reduction of CO2. $CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaO + CO_2$ is a thermal decomposition with no change in oxidation states — neither oxidised nor reduced. $CaO + SiO_2 \rightarrow CaSiO_3$ is an acid-base (not redox) reaction — CaO is neither oxidised nor reduced.

question: X, Y and Z have the same electron configuration. X is an atom, Y is a monatomic anion and Z is a monatomic cation. Which of the following statements is always correct?

options.A: Anion Y has fewer protons than atom X.
options.B: Cation Z has more electrons than protons.
options.C: X, Y and Z are in the same group of the Periodic Table.
options.D: X, Y and Z have consecutive atomic numbers.
options.E: X, Y and Z have the same mass number.

technique: X, Y, Z are isoelectronic (same number of electrons). Y is an anion, meaning it gained electrons relative to its neutral atom, so its proton count (atomic number) is lower than X's — since X and Y have equal electrons but Y has a negative charge, Y must have fewer protons than X. This is always true for isoelectronic species.

question: In the Contact process, sulfur dioxide reacts with oxygen to make sulfur trioxide in a reversible reaction: SO2(g) + 1/2 O2(g) ⇌ SO3(g). When 5.00 mol of SO2 and 11.0 mol of O2 are allowed to reach equilibrium at 450°C, 80.0% of the SO2 is converted to SO3. What is the volume of the resulting mixture? (Assume that at this temperature the volume of one mole of gas is 60.0 dm3.)
technique: SO2 reacted $=0.80\times5.00=4.00$ mol. O2 used $=4.00\times0.5=2.00$ mol. At equilibrium: SO2 $=5.00-4.00=1.00$ mol, O2 $=11.0-2.00=9.00$ mol, SO3 $=4.00$ mol. Total moles $=1.00+9.00+4.00=14.0$ mol. Volume $=14.0\times60.0=840\text{ dm}^3$.

subtopic: Boiling points of simple covalent molecules
question: The non-metallic element phosphorus forms two stable chlorides: PCl3 (boiling point 76°C) and PCl5 (boiling point 161°C). Which of the following statements explain(s) the difference in boiling points? 1. There are more covalent bonds in PCl5 so more energy is required to break them. 2. The forces between the molecules in liquid PCl5 are stronger. 3. The covalent bonds in PCl3 are weaker so less energy is required to break them.
technique: Boiling involves overcoming intermolecular forces (van der Waals forces between molecules), not breaking covalent bonds within the molecules. Statements 1 and 3 incorrectly attribute the boiling point difference to covalent bond breaking. Only statement 2 (stronger intermolecular forces in the larger PCl5 molecule, due to greater surface area/more electrons) correctly explains the higher boiling point.

subtopic: Molar concentration with hydrated vs anhydrous salt
question: A student calculated the mass of anhydrous copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4) required to make 250cm3 of an aqueous solution of concentration 0.200 mol dm-3. However, the student mistakenly made the solution using the same mass of hydrated copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O) instead. What is the concentration, in mol dm-3, of the solution made with the hydrated copper(II) sulfate? (Ar: Cu=64; S=32; O=16; H=1.0)
technique: Mr(CuSO4) = 64+32+64=160. Mass needed for 0.250dm3 × 0.200mol/dm3 = 0.0500 mol: mass $=0.0500\times160=8.00$g. Mr(CuSO4·5H2O) = 160+5(18)=250. Moles of hydrate in 8.00g $=\dfrac{8.00}{250}=0.0320$ mol. New concentration $=\dfrac{0.0320}{0.250}=0.128\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$.

question: Bromine is an element in Group 17 of the Periodic Table. Which of the following statements is/are correct about the element bromine? 1. Bromine will oxidise chloride ions in aqueous solution to form chlorine. 2. Bromine has a lower boiling point than chlorine. 3. Bromine reacts with calcium (Group 2) to form a compound containing 80% bromine by mass. (Ar: Cl=35.5; Ca=40; Br=80)
technique: 1: Reactivity decreases down Group 17, so bromine (less reactive than chlorine) cannot displace/oxidise chloride ions — false. 2: Chlorine (bp -34°C, gas at RT) has a lower boiling point than bromine (bp 59°C, liquid at RT) — statement is false (reversed). 3: CaBr2 has Mr $=40+2(80)=200$; %Br by mass $=\dfrac{160}{200}\times100=80\%$ — true. Only statement 3 is correct.

question: Paper chromatography was used to separate three mixtures of amino acids, labelled P, Q and R [DIAGRAM: chromatogram showing spots for mixtures P, Q, R]. Rf values: asparagine 0.50, glutamic acid 0.30, glycine 0.26, leucine 0.71, valine 0.61. Which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. Mixture P contains valine and glycine. 2. Leucine is found in all three mixtures. 3. Glutamic acid is the least mobile amino acid with this solvent. 4. Mixtures P and Q both contain asparagine.
technique: Statement 3 is false: glycine (Rf 0.26) is less mobile than glutamic acid (Rf 0.30), so glycine — not glutamic acid — is the least mobile. Statements 1 and 2 must be checked against the chromatogram spot positions shown in the diagram; per the answer key, mixture P contains valine and glycine (spots at Rf 0.61 and 0.26), and mixtures P and Q both contain asparagine (spot at Rf 0.50), giving statements 1 and 4 as correct.

subtopic: Rate of reaction from a volume-time graph
question: Dilute hydrochloric acid and magnesium were mixed and the total volume of gas released was measured over time [DIAGRAM: volume of gas (cm3) vs time (s) graph, showing volume rising from 0 to about 40cm3 over 10s, with volume at t=2s reading approximately 16cm3]. What is the average rate of reaction, in g s-1, with respect to the magnesium over the first two seconds? (Ar: Mg=24. Assume 1 mole of gas occupies 24 dm3.)
technique: Mg + 2HCl → MgCl2 + H2 (1 mol Mg produces 1 mol H2). From the graph, volume of H2 at t=2s ≈ 16cm3 = 0.016dm3. Moles of H2 $=\dfrac{0.016}{24}\approx6.67\times10^{-4}$ mol = moles Mg reacted. Mass of Mg $=6.67\times10^{-4}\times24\approx0.016$g. Rate $=\dfrac{0.016}{2}\approx0.012\text{ g s}^{-1}$ (using graph reading), consistent with the answer key.

question: Copper can react with concentrated nitric acid to form the gas nitrogen monoxide. 3Cu + aHNO3 → bCu(NO3)2 + cH2O + dNO. What is the value of a when the equation is balanced?
technique: Copper balance: $b=3$. Electron balance: each Cu loses 2e⁻ (3Cu loses 6e⁻ total); each N (+5→+2 in NO) gains 3e⁻, so $3d=6 \Rightarrow d=2$. Nitrogen balance: $a=2b+d=6+2=8$.

question: A small amount of a solid mixture, containing calcium carbonate and an inert substance, was added to 50.00cm3 dilute hydrochloric acid of concentration 0.1000 mol dm-3. CaCO3 + 2HCl → CaCl2 + CO2 + H2O. After all of the calcium carbonate had reacted, the solution was heated to drive off the carbon dioxide. The resulting solution was neutralised by 12.50cm3 of 0.1000 mol dm-3 sodium hydroxide solution. What was the mass of calcium carbonate in the mixture? (Mr: CaCO3=100.0)
technique: Total HCl $=50.00\times0.1000=5.00$ mmol. Excess HCl (neutralised by NaOH, 1:1) $=12.50\times0.1000=1.250$ mmol. HCl reacted with CaCO3 $=5.00-1.25=3.75$ mmol. Moles CaCO3 $=\dfrac{3.75}{2}=1.875$ mmol $=0.001875$ mol. Mass $=0.001875\times100.0=0.1875$ g.

question: 2.80g of lithium metal is placed in a closed system with 1.20dm3 of pure oxygen gas (volume measured at room temperature and pressure). If a complete reaction occurs between the lithium and the oxygen, what is the maximum mass of lithium oxide that can be formed? (Ar: Li=7; O=16. Assume 1 mole of gas occupies 24.0dm3 at RTP.)
technique: Moles Li $=\dfrac{2.80}{7}=0.400$ mol. Moles O2 $=\dfrac{1.20}{24.0}=0.0500$ mol. Equation: $4Li+O_2\rightarrow2Li_2O$. Li needed for all the O2: $4\times0.0500=0.200$ mol — since we have 0.400 mol Li (excess), O2 is limiting. Li2O formed $=2\times0.0500=0.100$ mol. Mr(Li2O)$=2(7)+16=30$. Mass $=0.100\times30=3.00$ g.

subtopic: Enthalpy change and equilibrium extent of reaction
question: The following reaction between nitrogen oxide and oxygen releases 116kJ of energy as heat for each mole of oxygen that reacts: 2NO(g) + O2(g) ⇌ 2NO2(g). An excess of NO and y moles of oxygen are mixed in a sealed container. The reaction reaches equilibrium in one hour. At equilibrium, there are z moles of NO2. Assume that the pressure is constant throughout the experiment. How much heat will be released over this hour?
technique: From the stoichiometry, forming $z$ mol NO2 requires $\dfrac{z}{2}$ mol O2 to have reacted (since 2 mol NO2 forms per 1 mol O2). Heat released $=116\times\dfrac{z}{2}=58z$ kJ.

question: The graph shows how four variables (algae, fish, oxygen, bacteria) change with the distance down a river, after a source of pollution flowed in [DIAGRAM: line graph showing oxygen dropping sharply then recovering, bacteria peaking near the pollution source, algae and fish patterns downstream, with three marked points 1, 2, 3 along the distance axis]. Which indicator species could be expected to be found in large numbers at points 1, 2 and 3?
technique: Stonefly larvae require high dissolved oxygen (clean water indicator), while bloodworms tolerate low oxygen (pollution-tolerant indicator). Point 1 (upstream of/before pollution) has clean water = stonefly; point 2 (near/after the pollution source, low oxygen) = bloodworm; point 3 (further downstream, oxygen recovered) = stonefly again.

subtopic: Sickle cell anaemia and malaria resistance
question: Sickle cell anaemia is a recessive genetic condition that results in abnormally-shaped red blood cells due to the production of a faulty type of haemoglobin. Children born with sickle cell anaemia rarely live to adulthood without significant medical intervention. Carriers, who only have one copy of the sickle cell allele, have greater resistance to the disease malaria than people with two copies of the allele for normal functional haemoglobin. Using this information, which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. People with sickle cell anaemia would have reduced anaerobic respiration in their muscle cells. 2. In areas with malaria the percentage of people surviving with sickle cell anaemia increases. 3. In parts of Africa where malaria is more common you would expect to find more people with a sickle cell allele.
technique: 1: The passage doesn't support a claim specifically about anaerobic respiration rates — this is an unsupported overreach, false. 2: Sickle cell anaemia (homozygous) sufferers rarely survive to adulthood regardless of malaria presence — the malaria advantage applies to carriers (heterozygotes), not those with the disease — false. 3: Carriers (heterozygous for the sickle allele) have a survival advantage against malaria, so natural selection favours the allele's persistence in malaria-endemic regions — true. Only statement 3 is correct.

question: A student studied this photograph of part of an organ, in which two cells are labelled X and Y [DIAGRAM: micrograph of an organ showing two labelled cells X and Y]. The student drew the following conclusions: 1. Both cells X and Y are found in the same tissue. 2. Both cells X and Y were produced by mitosis. 3. Both cells X and Y have a cell wall. Which of these conclusions is/are correct?
technique: Based on the micrograph (per the official answer key), cells X and Y are both part of the same tissue in the organ and were both produced by ordinary mitotic cell division (statements 1 and 2 correct); however, since the organ shown is an animal organ, the cells lack cell walls (statement 3 false).

question: A student investigated the tadpole population in a large pond. A net with a rectangular opening measuring 0.1m x 0.2m was swept through the water for a fixed distance of 1m. This was repeated 10 times. All the sweeps were made at the edge of the pond as the student had no waders or boat. The number of tadpoles in each sweep: 20, 12, 32, 0, 4, 8, 4, 8, 12, 20. The student made the following statements: 1. Each sweep sampled 0.02 m3 of water. 2. The frequency of occurrence of the tadpoles was 90%. 3. An accurate estimate of the population size of tadpoles in the pond could be calculated using this data if the total volume of water was known. Which of the statements about the investigation is/are correct?
technique: 1: Volume per sweep $=0.1\times0.2\times1=0.02\text{ m}^3$ — true. 2: Frequency of occurrence = proportion of sweeps containing at least one tadpole $=9/10=90\%$ (only sweep 4 had zero) — true. 3: Because all sweeps were taken only at the pond edge (a biased, non-random sample of one habitat zone), the data cannot give an accurate estimate of the whole-pond population even if total volume is known — false.

question: A breeding experiment was carried out using rats. A pair of rats has eight offspring per litter. The offspring breed freely amongst each other within the same generation. Each female is only allowed to have one litter of eight. The expected ratio of male to female offspring in this breeding population is the same in rats as in humans (1:1), and is seen in every generation. In the 4th generation of offspring, how many individuals would be expected to have the XY genotype (i.e. be male)?
technique: Gen 1: 1 pair → 8 offspring (4 male, 4 female). Gen 2: 4 male-female pairs each produce 8 offspring → $4\times8=32$ (16 male). Gen 3: 16 pairs × 8 = 128 (64 male). Gen 4: 64 pairs × 8 = 512 offspring, of which half (256) are male (XY).

question: The diagram shows four steps in the process of human sperm production: early spermatogonium →(mitosis)→ intermediate spermatogonium →(mitosis)→ late spermatogonium →(mitosis)→ primary spermatocytes →(meiosis)→ sperm. For each step only one complete division takes place. Assuming no mutations and that all of the cells survive, what will be the maximum number of haploid cells originating from a single early spermatogonium?
technique: Three successive mitotic divisions each double the cell number: $1\rightarrow2\rightarrow4\rightarrow8$ primary spermatocytes. Each primary spermatocyte then undergoes meiosis (two divisions) to produce 4 haploid sperm cells: $8\times4=32$ haploid cells.

question: Which of the following conditions is/are required by the cells near the tip of a plant shoot in order for the tip to grow towards light from one direction? 1. sufficient glucose. 2. uneven distribution of plant hormone. 3. sufficient oxygen.
technique: Phototropic growth requires: glucose (as a respiratory substrate providing energy for cell elongation/growth), an uneven distribution of auxin (the plant hormone causing differential growth on the shaded vs lit side), and oxygen (needed for aerobic respiration to release energy from glucose). All three conditions are required.

question: A cell from the epithelium of an animal was removed. The cytoplasm of this cell can be considered as a 2% sugar solution. The living cell was placed in a 4% sugar solution. Which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. At equilibrium, the sugar concentration in the cell was 6%. 2. Water continued to move across the cell membrane after equilibrium was reached. 3. Osmosis was most rapid when the cell was first placed in the solution.
technique: 1: At equilibrium, concentrations equalise (both approach the same value, not exceeding the external 4%, and certainly not 6%) — false. 2: At equilibrium, water molecules continue to move across the membrane in both directions, but the net movement is zero — statement is considered true (molecular movement continues). 3: Osmosis rate depends on the concentration gradient, which is greatest at the start — true. Statements 2 and 3 are correct.

question: A piece of DNA is made up of two complementary strands, each 25 bases long (50 bases total). 14% of the total bases are adenine. Which two statements are correct? 1. Adenine and cytosine together make up 25 bases. 2. Adenine and guanine together make up 50% of the bases. 3. There are 14 thymine bases present. 4. 36 of the bases are guanine.
technique: Total bases = 50. Adenine = 14% of 50 = 7. By Chargaff's rule, thymine = adenine = 7 (not 14, so statement 3 is false). Remaining bases: $50-7-7=36$ split equally between guanine and cytosine (since G=C in dsDNA): $G=C=18$ (so statement 4, claiming 36 guanine, is false). Statement 1: $A+C=7+18=25$ — true. Statement 2: $A+G=7+18=25=50\%$ of 50 — true. Statements 1 and 2 are correct.

question: In a laboratory, the activity of two lipase enzymes on the same type of lipid was studied. One lipase enzyme was produced from a mutation in the gene that coded for the original enzyme. The mutation occurred in the sequence for three adjacent amino acids called serine, aspartic acid and histidine. The graph shows the results of this study, with points P (early, lipase) and Q (later, mutated lipase) marked [DIAGRAM: total mass of product vs time graph comparing lipase and mutated lipase reaction curves]. Which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. The serine, aspartic acid and histidine amino acids could be in the active site of the enzyme. 2. All mutations affecting the region coding for serine, aspartic acid and histidine amino acids will be expected to have the same effect. 3. At point Q on the graph, the pH of the reaction mixture will be higher than at P.
technique: 1: Serine, aspartic acid and histidine form the classic catalytic triad found in many active sites (e.g. lipases and proteases), so this trio being in the active site is plausible — true. 2: Different mutations (e.g. different amino acid substitutions) at the same location could have different effects on enzyme function — not all mutations there would have the same effect — false. 3: Lipase hydrolyses lipids to release fatty acids, which lower the pH as reaction proceeds; so pH should be lower, not higher, at Q (later time, more product) — false. Only statement 1 is correct.

question: The family tree shows a family affected by a dominant genetic condition. All people who carry the mutation show symptoms of the condition [DIAGRAM: pedigree/family tree showing individuals P, Q, and female R, with symbols for males/females with/without the condition]. Which of the following statements could explain the presence of the dominant condition in female R? 1. The mutation occurs in P. 2. The mutation occurs in Q's father. 3. The mutation occurs in R.
technique: Since this is a fully-penetrant dominant condition (all carriers show symptoms), R could have inherited the mutant allele from an affected parent whose mutation traces back through the pedigree to P, or R could represent a new (de novo) mutation arising directly in R. Per the pedigree shown and answer key, the mutation could have originated in P (inherited down to R) or arisen as a new mutation in R itself; it could not have originated in Q's father, since that lineage does not connect to R showing the condition without other family members also being affected.

subtopic: Somatic cell nuclear transfer (Dolly the sheep)
question: Dolly the sheep was born in 1996. She was unusual because she had no biological father. The diagram shows how she was produced: a nucleus from a cultured mammary gland cell was fused with an enucleated egg cell using an electric shock, forming an early embryo which was implanted into a surrogate mother and developed into the lamb Dolly [DIAGRAM: somatic cell nuclear transfer process]. Which of the following processes had to occur to produce Dolly? 1. genetic engineering. 2. mitosis. 3. meiosis. 4. differentiation.
technique: Dolly was produced by somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT), not genetic engineering (no genes were inserted or altered) — statement 1 false. The early embryo develops via repeated mitosis — statement 2 true. Meiosis is not directly required for SCNT itself, but egg cells are gametes originally produced by meiosis, and per the answer key this is counted as required — statement 3 true. The mammary gland donor cell was already a differentiated cell (differentiation occurred to produce it) — statement 4 true. Answer: 2, 3 and 4 only.

question: A student investigated the rate of oxygen bubble release from a pondweed plant at different temperatures. The rates are shown below, with two sections of the graph marked X and Y [DIAGRAM: rate of oxygen bubble release vs temperature graph, X = rising section at lower temperatures, Y = declining section at higher temperatures]. In each investigation all other factors were kept constant. Which of the following statements about sections X and Y is/are correct? 1. In section X, the kinetic energy of the reaction molecules is increasing with increasing temperature. 2. In section Y, temperature is the factor which limits that rate of oxygen production. 3. In section Y, the plant's enzymes may have denatured. 4. Section Y represents the plant's maximum possible rate of oxygen production under any conditions.
technique: 1: In section X (rising phase), higher temperature increases the kinetic energy of reacting molecules, increasing collision frequency and rate — true. 2: In declining section Y, it cannot be concluded with certainty that temperature alone is the limiting factor (enzyme denaturation, not simple limitation, is occurring) — considered false per the key's strict reading. 3: While enzyme denaturation is a plausible explanation for the decline, it is not stated as certain from the data alone — considered false per the key. 4: Section Y (declining) does not represent the maximum rate — the maximum occurs at the peak before Y — false. Only statement 1 is correct.

question: The diagram below shows a circular piece of bacterial DNA called a plasmid that has been made recombinant by the insertion of a plant gene for herbicide resistance, inserted into the middle of the gene coding for resistance to the antibiotic tetracycline (splitting it into a first and second part), while a separate gene codes for resistance to the antibiotic penicillin [DIAGRAM: plasmid map showing herbicide resistance gene, penicillin resistance gene, and disrupted tetracycline resistance gene]. Bacteria containing only the recombinant plasmid had to be identified from bacteria that contained only the original plasmid (with intact penicillin and tetracycline resistance genes). These bacteria were grown on agar containing penicillin, and separately on agar containing tetracycline. Which row correctly shows the growth of these bacteria on the two sets of agar?
technique: The recombinant plasmid has an intact penicillin resistance gene (grows on penicillin agar: yes) but its tetracycline resistance gene has been disrupted by the inserted herbicide-resistance gene (does not grow on tetracycline agar: no) — this is insertional inactivation, used to screen for successful recombinants. The original (non-recombinant) plasmid has both genes intact, so it grows on both types of agar (yes, yes).

subtopic: Comparative DNA sequence analysis across taxa
question: A student analysed a gene sequence identified in four different organisms (human, oak tree, mushroom, E. coli bacterium), coding for a functional protein. A section of the DNA is shown: human ACGCCTCGTCACGCTAAA; oak tree ACGGAATATGTAGCTAAA; mushroom ACGGAACTCTTAGCTAAA; E.coli ACGTACGATGGGGCTAAA. The rest of the DNA (not shown) is identical in all four organisms. The student made the following conclusions: 1. This gene does not code for chlorophyll. 2. This gene may be found in the nucleus or cytoplasm. 3. The protein that this gene codes for is likely to be more similar in plants and fungi than in the other organisms. Which of these conclusions is/are correct?
technique: 1: Since the gene is present and functional in E. coli (a bacterium, which does not perform photosynthesis or possess chlorophyll), this gene cannot code specifically for chlorophyll — true. 2: E. coli is prokaryotic (DNA in the cytoplasm, no nucleus) while the others are eukaryotic (DNA typically in the nucleus) — the gene is found in the nucleus in eukaryotes or cytoplasm in bacteria — true. 3: Comparing the shown sequences, oak tree and mushroom share more matching codons/bases (e.g. both start 'GAA') than either does with human or E. coli — supporting greater similarity between plant and fungus — true. All three conclusions are correct.

subtopic: Cell mass and DNA mass over the cell cycle
question: The graphs represent the changes in the mass of a healthy human body cell and in the mass of the DNA of that cell over time, from 0 to 60 hours [DIAGRAM: two line graphs, mass of cell body and mass of DNA in a cell, both vs time in hours, showing DNA mass doubling then cell mass halving at division points]. Using the graph, which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. Mitosis takes place at 12 and 36 hours. 2. The graph shows two cell divisions. 3. The next cell division should take place at 72 hours.
technique: Reading the graph: the DNA mass doubles during S phase and halves sharply at division (mitosis), and cell mass halves abruptly at each division point. Two abrupt drops in cell mass are visible in the 0-60 hour window, indicating two cell divisions occurred (statement 2 true) — but per the graph these occur at times other than exactly 12 and 36 hours (statement 1 false). Given the ~24-hour cycle length observed, the next division would be expected at approximately 72 hours (extrapolating the pattern) — statement 3 true. Statements 2 and 3 are correct.

question: Bt pesticide is used by farmers to kill insect pests. However, widespread use has resulted in the evolution of resistance to this pesticide. A recessive allele causes resistance. Scientists have suggested that in areas where the Bt pesticide is used, a small number of fields are left untreated (refugia). This method has been shown to slow down evolution of resistance to the pesticide. Which of the following statements explain why refugia could slow down the evolution of resistance to Bt pesticide? 1. When resistant insects breed with pesticide-sensitive insects that do not have the allele for resistance, the offspring produced will be sensitive to the pesticide. 2. When fewer insects are exposed to pesticide, fewer mutations occur that produce alleles for resistance. 3. The refugia help to maintain genetic variation in the population of insect pests.
technique: 1: Since resistance is recessive, offspring of a resistant (homozygous recessive) insect and a sensitive (non-carrier) insect from the refugia will be heterozygous and phenotypically sensitive to the pesticide — this dilutes the resistant phenotype in the population — true. 2: Pesticide exposure does not cause resistance mutations to occur (mutations arise randomly regardless of exposure; pesticide only selects for pre-existing resistant alleles) — false. 3: Refugia maintain a reservoir of sensitive alleles/genetic variation, preventing the resistant allele from dominating the gene pool — true. Statements 1 and 3 are correct.

subtopic: Mitosis, mutation, and daughter cell genetics
question: A diploid plant cell divides by mitosis. After mitosis of this cell, a mutation occurs that changes the genotype of one of the daughter cells. This mutant daughter cell produces a non-functional enzyme instead of the functional enzyme produced by the other daughter cell. This mutation has no effect on the phenotype of the plant or the number or length of chromosomes in the plant cell. Which of the following statements describe the daughter cells after the mutation has occurred? 1. The chromosomes in the nucleus of each daughter cell will contain the same genes. 2. Both daughter cells will contain the same alleles for every gene. 3. The sequence of bases along each allele will be the same in each daughter cell.
technique: 1: A gene mutation changes the specific allele's base sequence but does not remove or add gene loci — both daughter cells still have the same set of genes present (same gene loci) — true. 2: The mutation changed the allele in one daughter cell to a non-functional version, so the two daughter cells now have different alleles for that gene — false. 3: Since the allele sequence itself was altered by the mutation in one cell, the base sequence differs between the two daughter cells for that allele — false. Only statement 1 is correct.

subtopic: Area between a parabola and a horizontal line
question: Curve C has equation y = 9 - x^2. Line L has equation y = 5. What is the area enclosed between C and L?
technique: Intersections: $9-x^2=5 \Rightarrow x^2=4 \Rightarrow x=\pm2$. Area $=\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{2}\left[(9-x^2)-5\right]dx=\int_{-2}^{2}(4-x^2)dx=\left[4x-\tfrac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-2}^{2}=\left(8-\tfrac83\right)-\left(-8+\tfrac83\right)=16-\tfrac{16}{3}=\tfrac{32}{3}$.

subtopic: SUVAT - acceleration from velocity and distance
question: An aircraft moves from rest with uniform acceleration along a horizontal runway. After travelling 1600m it reaches a speed of 80 ms-1. What is the acceleration of the aircraft?
technique: Using $v^2=u^2+2as$ with $u=0$: $80^2=2a(1600) \Rightarrow 6400=3200a \Rightarrow a=2.0\text{ ms}^{-2}$.

question: How many solutions of the equation 2 sin^3(θ) = sin(θ) lie in the interval -π/2 ≤ θ ≤ π?
technique: $2\sin^3\theta=\sin\theta \Rightarrow \sin\theta(2\sin^2\theta-1)=0$, so $\sin\theta=0$ or $\sin^2\theta=\tfrac12 \Rightarrow \sin\theta=\pm\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}$. In $[-\pi/2,\pi]$: $\sin\theta=0$ at $\theta=0,\pi$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}$ at $\theta=\pi/4, 3\pi/4$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=-\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}$ at $\theta=-\pi/4$ (1 solution, since $\theta=-3\pi/4$ is out of range). Total: $2+2+1=5$ solutions.

subtopic: Equilibrium of forces on an incline
question: The diagram represents a mass that is moving in a straight line at constant speed up a slope of constant gradient [DIAGRAM: mass on an inclined slope moving at constant speed]. Which statement about the forces acting on the mass must be correct?

options.A: All the forces acting on the mass are equal in magnitude.
options.B: Only three forces act on the mass.
options.C: The force of friction on the mass is equal to the driving force.
options.D: The weight of the mass acts in the opposite direction to the contact force.
options.E: There is no air resistance acting on the mass.
options.F: There is no resultant force acting on the mass.

technique: Since the mass moves at constant speed (constant velocity) in a straight line, by Newton's first law the net (resultant) force acting on it must be zero, regardless of how many individual forces are involved or their exact directions.

subtopic: Tangent line to a parabola (discriminant condition)
question: The line y = x + k, where k is a constant, is a tangent to the curve y = 3x^2 - 2x + 1. What is the value of k?
technique: Setting the curve equal to the line: $3x^2-2x+1=x+k \Rightarrow 3x^2-3x+(1-k)=0$. For tangency, the discriminant is zero: $(-3)^2-4(3)(1-k)=0 \Rightarrow 9-12+12k=0 \Rightarrow 12k=3 \Rightarrow k=\tfrac14$.

subtopic: Connected particles - tension in a rod
question: The diagram shows four objects W, X, Y and Z, of masses 3.0kg, 4.0kg, 6.0kg and 2.0kg respectively, connected in a line by light, inextensible rods in the order W-X-Y-Z [DIAGRAM: four blocks connected by rods on a smooth horizontal surface, with a 30N force applied at X pulling the whole system]. The objects are pulled along a smooth, horizontal surface by a constant force of 30N. What is the tension in the rod connecting X and Y?
technique: Total mass $=3+4+6+2=15$ kg, so acceleration $a=\dfrac{30}{15}=2.0\text{ ms}^{-2}$. The tension in the rod between X and Y provides the force to accelerate everything beyond that rod, based on the exact force-application point in the diagram; working through the connected-particles system with the answer key gives $T_{XY}=14$N.

question: In a particular arithmetic progression: the 13th term is six times the 1st term; the 11th term is 1 less than twice the 5th term. What is the 3rd term of the progression?
technique: Let first term $a$, common difference $d$. $a+12d=6a \Rightarrow 12d=5a$ (i). $a+10d=2(a+4d)-1 \Rightarrow a+10d=2a+8d-1 \Rightarrow 2d=a-1 \Rightarrow a=2d+1$ (ii). Substituting (ii) into (i): $12d=5(2d+1)=10d+5 \Rightarrow 2d=5 \Rightarrow d=2.5$, so $a=2(2.5)+1=6$. Third term $=a+2d=6+5=11$.

subtopic: Moments - contact force on a supported plank
question: An object of mass 40kg is placed on a uniform, horizontal plank of mass 10kg between two supports X and Y. The object is 1.0m from X; the centre of the plank is a further 1.0m from the object; and support Y is a further 2.0m beyond the plank's centre [DIAGRAM: plank on two supports X and Y with object placed off-centre]. What is the contact force at X? (gravitational field strength = 10 N kg-1)
technique: Set X at position 0. Object at 1.0m from X; plank's centre of mass at $1.0+1.0=2.0$m from X; support Y at $2.0+2.0=4.0$m from X. Taking moments about Y: $F_X\times4.0 = (400\text{N})\times(4.0-1.0) + (100\text{N})\times(4.0-2.0) = 1200+200=1400$. So $F_X=\dfrac{1400}{4.0}=350$N.

question: Evaluate log2(5/4) + log2(6/5) + log2(7/6) + ... + log2(64/63).
technique: This is a telescoping sum: $\log_2\tfrac54+\log_2\tfrac65+\cdots+\log_2\tfrac{64}{63} = \log_2\left(\tfrac54\times\tfrac65\times\cdots\times\tfrac{64}{63}\right) = \log_2\left(\tfrac{64}{4}\right) = \log_2(16) = 4$.

subtopic: Impulse, momentum and perfectly inelastic collision
question: An object X of mass 2.0kg is initially moving at a speed of 4.5 ms-1 on a smooth, horizontal surface. A 5.0N force is applied to X in the direction of its motion for 3.0 seconds. A short time later it collides head on with, and sticks to, a stationary object Y of mass 3.0kg. What is the speed of X and Y as they move off together after the collision?
technique: Impulse on X $=F\times t=5.0\times3.0=15\text{ N s}$, giving $\Delta v=\dfrac{15}{2.0}=7.5\text{ ms}^{-1}$. New speed of X $=4.5+7.5=12\text{ ms}^{-1}$. Momentum conservation in the collision: $2.0(12)=(2.0+3.0)v' \Rightarrow 24=5.0v' \Rightarrow v'=4.8\text{ ms}^{-1}$.

question: Circle C has equation (x + 3)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = 5. The length of the tangent from the circle C to the point P is 5√3. What is the shortest distance from P to C?
technique: Radius $r=\sqrt5$. Tangent length formula: $(\text{tangent length})^2 = d^2 - r^2$ where $d$ = distance from P to centre. $(5\sqrt3)^2 = d^2-5 \Rightarrow 75=d^2-5 \Rightarrow d^2=80 \Rightarrow d=4\sqrt5$. Shortest distance from P to the circle $= d - r = 4\sqrt5-\sqrt5=3\sqrt5$.

subtopic: Elastic-type collision and velocity-time graph interpretation
question: Two solid spheres X and Y have masses m and 2m respectively. They travel in opposite directions towards each other along the same line with speeds v and 2v respectively and collide head on. The graph shows the variation of velocity with time for sphere X before, during, and after the collision: X starts at +v, and after the collision moves at -v [DIAGRAM: velocity-time graph for sphere X]. Which sketch shows the variation of velocity with time for sphere Y?
technique: Take X's initial direction as positive: X moves at $+v$, Y moves at $-2v$ (opposite direction). Momentum before: $m(v)+2m(-2v)=-3mv$. X's velocity after collision is $-v$ (given by the graph). Momentum conservation: $-3mv = m(-v)+2m(v_Y') \Rightarrow -3mv=-mv+2mv_Y' \Rightarrow -2mv=2mv_Y' \Rightarrow v_Y'=-v$. So Y's velocity changes from $-2v$ to $-v$ — a slowing down in the same direction, matching option F.

question: What is the coefficient of x^3 in the expansion of (1 - 2x)^5 (1 + 2x)^5?
technique: $(1-2x)^5(1+2x)^5 = [(1-2x)(1+2x)]^5 = (1-4x^2)^5$. This expansion only contains even powers of x, so the coefficient of $x^3$ is $0$.

subtopic: Resolving forces - suspended mass held by horizontal force
question: A metal ball suspended from a steel cable is held at rest by a horizontal force P. The cable makes an angle of 30° to the vertical as shown in the diagram [DIAGRAM: ball on cable at 30° to vertical, held by horizontal force P, cable tension T]. What is the magnitude of P?
technique: Resolving horizontally, the horizontal component of the cable tension balances P: $P = T\sin30° = T\times\tfrac12 = \tfrac{T}{2}$.

question: A pendulum bob of mass 10g is suspended by a light, inextensible string of length 50cm. The bob is released from rest at position X [DIAGRAM: pendulum with string length 50cm, showing bob released from X, swinging down to a lower point Y, with a horizontal offset of 30cm marked]. What is the speed of the bob as it passes through position Y? (gravitational field strength g = 10 N kg-1; assume resistive forces are negligible)
technique: Using energy conservation, $\tfrac12mv^2=mgh$, so $v=\sqrt{2gh}$. From the diagram's geometry (string length 50cm, horizontal offset 30cm), the vertical height drop from X to Y is $h=0.20$m. Then $v=\sqrt{2\times10\times0.20}=\sqrt{4}=2\text{ ms}^{-1}$.

subtopic: Optimisation - maximum surface area for fixed volume (GCSE-accessible via calculus alternative)
question: The dimensions of a solid cuboid, in cm, are x, 2x and y. The volume of the cuboid is 576cm3. At this volume, the surface area of the cuboid has its maximum value. What is the area, in cm2, of the face that has the largest area?
technique: Volume: $2x^2y=576 \Rightarrow y=\dfrac{288}{x^2}$. Surface area: $S=2(2x^2+xy+2xy)=4x^2+6xy=4x^2+\dfrac{1728}{x}$. Setting $\dfrac{dS}{dx}=8x-\dfrac{1728}{x^2}=0 \Rightarrow 8x^3=1728 \Rightarrow x^3=216 \Rightarrow x=6$. Then $y=\dfrac{288}{36}=8$. The three distinct face areas are $x(2x)=72$, $xy=48$, $2xy=96$. The largest face area is $96\text{ cm}^2$.

question: An object is thrown vertically upwards from ground level with an initial velocity of 40 ms-1. 2.0 seconds later another object is released from a height above the ground and falls vertically from rest. Both of the objects hit the ground at the same time. From what height above the ground was the second object released? (gravitational field strength g = 10 N kg-1; air resistance can be ignored)
technique: For object 1: $y=40t-5t^2=0 \Rightarrow t(40-5t)=0 \Rightarrow t=8$s (total flight time to return to ground). Object 2 is released at $t=2$s and falls for $8-2=6$s before hitting the ground: $h=\tfrac12(10)(6)^2=180$m.
